content:
A Competition So Expensive That Winning Might Be Losing

11 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Androids in line — Photo by julien Tromeur on UnsplashThe strangest thing about the AI industry is that nobody seems to be enjoying winning.
Every week, another company announces a breakthrough, raises another billion dollars, buys another warehouse full of chips, and then immediately explains why this still isn’t enough.
It’s the only race where crossing the finish line is followed by a press release saying, “Unfortunately, we have discovered another finish line.”
And everyone applauds.
Because apparently we are now participating in a global competition where the official strategy is: spend more money than God, faster than your competitors, while publicly insisting that slowing down would be irresponsible.
Which is an unusual definition of responsibility.
The modern AI industry has become something that resembles a marathon, a casino, a nuclear arms race, and a Black Friday sale at Costco all happening simultaneously in a server farm the size of Luxembourg.
And the most remarkable part?
The people running it are increasingly honest about what’s happening.
